hook: You already know that streets can run side by side without ever crossing, or meet at a perfect corner — and those everyday observations are exactly what geometry formalizes. What you don't yet have are the tools to *prove* those relationships and use them to solve problems. In this unit, you'll discover how two simple ideas — lines that never meet and lines that meet at right angles — unlock a powerful system of angle relationships, proofs, and algebraic connections.
concept: Every topic in this unit flows from one central idea: the precise geometric and algebraic relationships that arise when lines are parallel or perpendicular. Formally, we study the properties of lines with equal slopes ($m_1 = m_2$) and negative reciprocal slopes ($m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$), connecting visual geometry to coordinate algebra.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to identify parallel lines, perpendicular lines, and transversals in a diagram and name the angle pairs they create — building the vocabulary you'll use throughout the entire unit.
Master the relationships between corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and co-interior angles so you can find missing angle measures whenever a transversal cuts parallel lines.
Apply converse theorems to write logical proofs and determine, from angle evidence alone, whether two lines in a figure must be parallel.
You will learn how perpendicular lines always form $90°$ angles and use theorems about perpendicularity — such as the uniqueness of a perpendicular through a point — to solve geometric problems.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Slopes of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
topic_roadmap.description: Discover how to use slope to classify pairs of lines on the coordinate plane, recognizing that parallel lines share equal slopes and perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Writing Equations of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
topic_roadmap.description: Apply slope relationships to write the equation of a line that passes through a given point and is parallel or perpendicular to a given line, connecting geometric reasoning directly to algebra.
